driver.body: Density that loses money is a countdown, not a moat. This driver asks a single question — is each incremental interaction accretive? — and it answers with the vehicle itself. The sensor stack that makes autonomy possible is also the cost line that decides the flywheel's terminal contribution, so **mobility moves D only insofar as the per-ride economics let Kepler hold the density rather than retreat from it** [S-017]. Every mechanism below is that question resolved into hardware and into the number the flywheel is chartered to move.

mechanisms.body:
The AV is a rolling sensor platform, and its bill of materials is the largest single lever on contribution per interaction — the swing variable that decides whether density is a moat or a countdown [S-018]. The teardown is not a parts list; it is the cost thesis exploded outward from the chassis, and it maps one-to-one to the safety timeline. The Q4 rain incident's fix — radar-weighted fusion — is precisely why the 4D imaging radar count is five, not three [S-113]. Reliability, in other words, is priced into the BOM.

Every one of these sensors is co-designed with Devices (D5), and their raw streams are the proprietary telemetry that widens Data breadth (B) for D6's models. Mobility manufactures D, but the vehicle is the exact point where it hands B to the platform — the sensor is simultaneously a cost line and a data faucet [S-114].

The BOM makes the swing visible in one view. **Lidar is 61% of the sensor bill and the entire cost thesis**: the solid-state roadmap that roughly halves the stack to ~$6,900 is what flips per-ride contribution positive at current density [S-115]. The trade-off is timing — deploy density now on expensive lidar and eat negative contribution, or wait for cheap lidar and cede the density lead.

Kepler deploys now, because D compounds and cost is a schedule. A density lead surrendered to save eighteen months of lidar depreciation is a lead a rival, having filled the OD tensor first, may never return. Cost curves bend on a roadmap that engineering controls; density leads, once lost, must be re-bought against an incumbent's better models — an asymmetry that makes the expensive-lidar years a defensible investment rather than a subsidy to be apologised for [S-115].

The number that ties this domain to the bible's central promise is blended contribution per interaction, which the flywheel is chartered to lift from −0.31 over ten years [S-019]. Mobility drives its share of that swing through two chained effects. First, density raises utilization (Driver 1), which amortises the vehicle across more paid minutes. Second, the sensor stream widens B (this driver), which sharpens the fraud, routing, and siting models that lower cost across D2, D3, and D4.

The distinction that matters: the mechanism moves D's value, not its count — the same interaction is worth more once four other domains monetise it. The cross-feed made concrete: **mobility's trajectory-plus-device-fingerprint stream is the strongest single input to Payments (D4) fraud scoring**, and each fraud dollar D4 saves is booked partly against the ride that generated the signal [S-116]. A ride, priced once, is paid for many times across the flywheel.

The edge case sharpens the logic. The loss-leader ride — a deliberately under-priced trip in a ramping zone, the Reforma Corridor — carries negative contribution on its own line, but its data closes a coverage gap in the OD tensor. It is an investment in D justified only by the flywheel, never by the ride's own P&L [S-117]. Judged as a standalone fare it is a mistake; judged as a moat input it is the cheapest breadth Kepler can buy.
